content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 18, 1988


Craig R. Nealis
Deputy City Administrator
City of Bellflower
16600 Civic Center Drive
Bellflower, CA 90706-5494

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our Advice File No. I-88-368

Dear Mr. Nealis:

We have received your letter regarding the prohibition of
publicly funded newsletters and mass mailings under the
Political Reform Act.1/ Your letter states only a general
question. Therefore, we consider it to be a request for
informal assistance pursuant to Commission Regulation 18329(c)
(copy enclosed).2/

The advice is based upon emergency Regulation 18901 which
was adopted by the Commission in July. A public hearing on
permanent Regulation 18901 will be held on December 6, 1988.
The permanent regulation may alter our advice. A copy of the
notice and text of that permanent regulation is enclosed.

QUESTION

You have requested clarification of the restriction on
newsletters and other mass mailings sent at public expense.
Specifically, you are interested in restrictions on the city's
mass mailings when the names of elected officials may appear in
the letterhead or a roster.

___________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Craig R. Nealis
November 18, 1988
Page -2-


CONCLUSION

Emergency Regulation Section 18901 provides that a mailing
is not prohibited if it is mailed by an elected officer's
agency, on the agency's official stationery, forms and
envelopes, and the elected officer's name only appears on the
standard letterhead or logotype of the stationery, forms or
envelopes. There must be no other references to the officer,
including his or her photograph or signature, in the mailing.

ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 89001 was amended by Proposition
73, the campaign finance reform initiative on the June 1988
statewide primary ballot. Section 89001 provides that no
newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense.

Section 82041.5 defines "mass mailing." Section 82041.5
was also amended by Proposition 73, to provide:

"Mass mailing" means two hundred or more
substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not
include a form letter or mailing which is sent in
response to an unsolicited request, letter or other
inquiry."

The Commission, at its July 26, 1988 meeting, adopted
emergency Regulation 18901 (copy enclosed) which permits the
mailing of certain newsletters or mass mailings at public
expense. It was the commission's belief that the voters did
not intend to prohibit essential governmental mailings, but
rather to prevent elected officers from using governmental
newsletters or other mass mailings to promote their own
incumbency.

Regulation 18901 provides that a mailing is not prohibited
under Section 89001 if:

(1) It is mailed by an elected officer's agency;

(2) The stationery, forms and envelopes used for the
mailing are the official stationery, forms and envelopes
of the agency; and

(3) The elected officer's name appears, if at all,
only on the standard letterhead or logotype of the
stationery, forms or envelopes and there are not other

Craig R. Nealis
November 18, 1988
Page -3-


references to the officer, including his or her photograph
or signature, in the mailing.

(Regulation 18901(b).)

The emergency regulation permits the name of an elected
officer to be included in the standard letterhead or logotype
of the agency's official stationery. An example would be the
manner your city's stationery lists its elected officers. An
elected officer's photograph, signature or other reference
would be prohibited.

An example of the use of a "logotype" would be a
publication such as a community college district's schedule of
classes. The schedule will typically contain the name of logo
of the college and a list or roster of its superintendent,
trustees, registrar, dean of instruction, etc. Regulation
18901 permits such a publication to be sent at public expense.
Again, no photographs, signatures or other reference to an
elected officer are permitted.

We have advised agencies that the individualized stationery
of the elected officer is not the standard letterhead of the
agency. An elected officer's name can only appear on the
standard official letterhead of the city, not that which is the
personalized stationery of the elected officer.

The Commission will be considering a permanent regulation
on newsletters and mass mailings at its December 6, 1988
meeting. That regulation may provide more flexibility in the
types of mass mailings which are permitted.

I trust this letter responds to your question. Please feel
free to call if you have any specific questions or wish to
discuss this further.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

DMG:JET:aa

Enclosures

KEN CLEVELAND

WJ "BILL" PENDLETON

CITY OF
MAYOR

JOHN ANSDELL

BELLFLOWER

COUNCILMAN

RANDY BOMGAARS

16600 CIVIC CENTER DRIVE
BELLFLOWER CALIFORNIA 90706 5494

COUNCILMAN

JOSEPH CVETKO

(213) 804-1424

COUNCILMAN


September 20, 1988


Ms. Jeanette Turvill
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ms. Turvill:

We recently received information from the League of California Cities
regarding Proposition 73's effects on Section 89001 of the California
Government Code as it relates to the mailing of newsletters or other mass
mailings at public expense.

However, there appears to be some confusion. We respectfully request a
written response as to the methods in which rosters or names of elected
officials names can be used in newsletters or other mass mailings sent at
public expense.

We appreciate any information you can provide regarding this issue. Should
you require any additional information, please do not hesitate to call.
Thank you for your help. Best wishes and best regards from the "Friendly
City of Bellflower."

Sincerely,

Jack A. Simpson
City Administrator

Craig R. Nealis
Deputy City Administrator
City of Bellflower

Dear Ms. Turvill:

We recently received information from the League of California Cities
regarding Proposition 73's effects on Section 89001 of the California
Government Code as it relates to the mailing of newsletters or other mass
mailings at public expense.

However, there appears to be some confusion. We respectfully request a
written response as to the methods in which rosters or names of elected
officials names can be used in newsletters or other mass mailings sent at
public expense.

We appreciate any information you can provide regarding this issue. Should
you require any additional information, please do not hesitate to call.
Thank you for your help. Best wishes and best regards from the "Friendly
City of Bellflower."

Sincerely,

Jack A. Simpson
City Administrator

Craig R. Nealis
Deputy City Administrator
City of Bellflower
